doctorReport.packageReadme:
HealthLog health record

This package contains:
- report.pdf: the health report for your doctor.
- bundle.json: the same data as an HL7 FHIR R4 document Bundle, ePA-compatible.

Generated with HealthLog.

dialog.title: Enable Research Mode
dialog.intro: Research Mode adds an estimated drug-level chart to your GLP-1 medication page. Read the boundary below before turning it on — the chart is a learning aid, never a measurement, never medical advice.
dialog.whatItIs: A sawtooth curve simulating how your logged doses superimpose over time, using the one-compartment Bateman model with absorption and half-life parameters published in the EMA EPAR for each approved GLP-1 receptor agonist. The vertical axis is unit-less and unlabeled. The shape, not the number, is what the chart shows.
dialog.whatItIsnt: Not a blood level. Not a measurement. Not a forecast of how your body actually responds. Not a basis for any dose, timing, or escalation decision. Talk to your prescribing clinician for anything that affects how you take your medication.
dialog.whyEstimate: Population pharmacokinetics describe the typical patient. Individual variation is large — tirzepatide's Schneck/Urva 2024 model reports ~22% inter-individual variability on absorption and ~49% on volume of distribution. A textbook curve cannot capture your weight, kidney function, injection technique, or timing accuracy. Read the curve as the rhythm of dosing, not as your level.
dialog.mdrBoundary: HealthLog displays public regulatory data and your own log entries. It does not predict, diagnose, treat, or recommend any therapy. EU Regulation 2017/745 (the Medical Device Regulation, MDR) and guidance document MDCG 2021-24 draw the line at software that interprets data for medical purposes — this chart stays decisively on the educational side of that line. If you ever feel it's nudging you toward a clinical decision, close it.
dialog.citations: EMA European Public Assessment Reports for Mounjaro, Ozempic, Wegovy, Saxenda, Trulicity, Byetta, and Bydureon (Section 5.2). Schneck K, Urva S. Population pharmacokinetics of the GIP/GLP receptor agonist tirzepatide. CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol 2024;13(3):494-503 (DOI 10.1002/psp4.13099).
dialog.successToast: Research Mode enabled. The drug-level chart is now visible on your GLP-1 medication page.
dialog.errorToast: Could not enable Research Mode. Please try again.

explainer.bloodPressureBody: Blood pressure is the force your blood exerts on the artery walls, written as systolic over diastolic (the peak during a heartbeat over the resting value between beats). Consistently high readings strain the heart and blood vessels over time. As general guidance, European guidance (ESH 2023) calls under 120/80 optimal and 140/90 or above hypertension; US guidance (ACC/AHA 2017) sets a stricter line, labelling 130–139/80–89 as Stage 1. Categorise from a calm seated reading, never a single number.
explainer.pulseBody: Your pulse is how many times your heart beats per minute. A spot reading reflects what you were doing in the moment — at rest it is typically 60–100 beats per minute, and it rises naturally with movement, stress, or caffeine.
explainer.weightBody: Body weight is one of the simplest signals of long-term energy balance and overall health. The trend over weeks matters far more than any single reading, which fluctuates with hydration, food, and time of day.
explainer.bmiBody: BMI is your weight divided by your height squared, giving a single number that places you in a broad weight category. It is a population-level screen, not a diagnosis, and does not distinguish muscle from fat.
explainer.sleepBody: Sleep duration is how long you actually slept across the night, split into light, deep, and REM stages. Most adults need roughly seven to nine hours; both consistent quantity and quality support recovery, mood, and metabolism.
explainer.moodBody: Mood tracking records how you feel over time on a simple scale. Logging it regularly reveals patterns — how sleep, activity, or medication line up with how you feel — that a single day never shows.
explainer.medicationsBody: Adherence is how reliably you take your medication as prescribed, shown here as the share of expected doses you actually logged. Steady intake is often what makes a treatment work as intended.
explainer.restingHrBody: Resting heart rate is your heart rate when you are completely at rest, usually measured overnight — distinct from your pulse during activity. A lower resting rate generally points to better cardiovascular fitness, and a rising trend can be an early sign of stress or illness. As general guidance, a normal adult resting rate is 60–100 beats per minute (AHA 2024); a trained heart can sit benignly at 40–60.
explainer.hrvBody: HRV is the tiny variation in time between consecutive heartbeats. Higher variability usually reflects a well-recovered, adaptable nervous system, while a sustained drop can signal fatigue, stress, or insufficient recovery.
explainer.oxygenSaturationBody: SpO₂ is the percentage of your red blood cells carrying oxygen. As general guidance, readings of 95–100% are normal at rest (StatPearls/NIH 2023); values that stay below 90% warrant attention, though brief nightly dips are common and above ~98% adds no benefit. Optical readers can over-read true saturation in people with darker skin (US FDA 2024), so a single near-normal reading is not a clearance — retake when warm and still if concerned.
explainer.bodyTemperatureBody: Body temperature reflects your core thermal state. As general guidance, the population mean is about 36.6 °C — not 37.0 °C — with a normal oral range of roughly 35.7–37.4 °C and a fever from 38.0 °C (J Gen Intern Med 2019). It swings about 0.5 °C across the day, and sites differ by up to ~1 °C, so tag where you measured. Overnight wrist readings track relative change, not a clinical fever value.
explainer.activeEnergyBody: Active energy is the calories you burn through movement, on top of the baseline your body uses at rest. It is a direct measure of how physically active your day was.
explainer.workoutsBody: Workouts are the structured exercise sessions your devices record, with type, duration, and energy. Tracking them shows how regular training accumulates over weeks and how it relates to your other health signals.
explainer.respiratoryRateBody: Respiratory rate is the number of breaths you take per minute. As general guidance, at rest most adults breathe 12–20 times a minute (American Lung Association); it is meaningful only truly at rest or asleep. A sustained rise of about +3 above your own nightly baseline can be an early illness signal — clinical escalation cutoffs are hospital tools, not self-alarms.
explainer.bodyWaterBody: Total body water is the share of your body weight made up of fluid, inside and around your cells. It shifts with hydration, food, and activity, so the trend over weeks tells you more than any single reading.
explainer.boneMassBody: Bone mass is the estimated weight of the mineral in your skeleton. Scale-based estimates are approximate, but a steady or rising trend supports long-term bone strength.
explainer.fatFreeMassBody: Fat-free mass is everything in your body that is not fat — muscle, bone, organs, and water. Holding or building it while losing weight usually signals healthier body composition.
explainer.fatMassBody: Fat mass is the total weight of fat in your body. Some fat is essential for hormones and energy storage, but too much — especially around the abdomen — raises long-term health risk.
explainer.muscleMassBody: Muscle mass is the estimated weight of the muscle in your body. More muscle supports strength, metabolism, and mobility, and it tends to need active effort to preserve as you age.
explainer.visceralFatBody: Visceral fat is the fat stored deep in the abdomen around your organs. Unlike fat under the skin, a high level is closely linked to heart disease and type 2 diabetes, so lower is generally better. The scale shows a unitless rating (about 1–12 is the healthy range, 13 and up elevated); this is a separate scale from the imaging cm² thresholds clinicians use and is not interchangeable with them.
explainer.leanBodyMassBody: Lean body mass is your total weight minus body fat — muscle, bone, organs, and water combined. Tracking it alongside weight shows whether changes come from muscle or from fat.
explainer.flightsClimbedBody: Flights climbed counts the floors you go up each day, with one flight roughly ten feet of elevation. It is a simple marker of incidental activity and lower-body effort.
explainer.walkingDistanceBody: Walking and running distance is the ground you cover on foot each day. It complements step count by reflecting how far you moved, not just how often you stepped.
explainer.walkingSteadinessBody: Walking steadiness estimates how stable and balanced your gait is, classed from OK to low. A lower level points to a higher chance of a fall and is worth discussing with a clinician.
explainer.walkingHeartRateBody: Walking heart rate is your average pulse during a steady walk. A lower walking rate over time often reflects improving cardiovascular fitness for the same effort.
explainer.walkingAsymmetryBody: Walking asymmetry is the share of time your steps are uneven between the left and right leg. A higher percentage can point to pain, injury, or a gait problem worth looking into.
explainer.doubleSupportTimeBody: Double support time is the share of each walking cycle when both feet touch the ground at once. A higher percentage usually means a more cautious, less stable gait.
explainer.stepLengthBody: Step length is the average distance from one footfall to the next. A shorter step over time can reflect fatigue, caution, or a change in mobility.
explainer.walkingSpeedBody: Walking speed is how fast you move on a level walk. It is a well-studied marker of overall health and mobility, and a steady or rising pace is a good sign.
explainer.pulseWaveVelocityBody: Pulse wave velocity measures how fast the pressure wave from each heartbeat travels along your arteries. A faster wave indicates stiffer arteries, which is linked to higher cardiovascular risk. As general guidance, European clinical reference flags carotid-femoral velocity above 10 m/s (ESC/ESH 2018) — but a consumer estimate is a proxy, not interchangeable with that clinical measurement.
explainer.vascularAgeBody: Vascular age estimates how old your arteries behave compared with your actual age, based on arterial stiffness. A vascular age below your real age suggests healthier blood vessels.
explainer.environmentalAudioBody: Environmental sound levels track the loudness of the world around you in decibels. Prolonged exposure above about 70 dB can damage hearing over time, so lower daily averages are safer.
explainer.headphoneAudioBody: Headphone audio levels track how loudly you listen through earbuds or headphones, in decibels. Keeping the volume down and taking breaks protects your hearing over the long run.
explainer.audioEventsBody: Loud sound events flag moments when noise around you reached a level that can harm hearing with enough exposure. Frequent events are a cue to seek quieter surroundings or hearing protection.
explainer.daylightBody: Time in daylight is how long you spend in outdoor light each day. Regular daylight supports a healthy sleep-wake rhythm and mood, and time outdoors is linked to eye health.
explainer.bloodGlucoseBody: Blood glucose is the amount of sugar in your blood, the body's main energy source. It rises after eating and settles between meals. As general guidance for adults without diabetes, a fasting reading under 100 mg/dL is normal, 100–125 is prediabetes, and 126 or above falls in the diabetes range (ADA 2024). A single high reading does not diagnose — a clinician confirms with a repeat test, and people managing diabetes use different, clinician-set targets.
explainer.skinTemperatureBody: Skin temperature here is the small nightly change in your wrist temperature from your own baseline, not an absolute fever reading. Shifts can track sleep, environment, illness, or menstrual-cycle phase.
explainer.cardioFitnessBody: VO₂ max is how much oxygen your body can use at peak effort — the best lab index of aerobic fitness, which wearables estimate indirectly. As general guidance, fitness is a graded predictor of survival: each one-MET (about 3.5 mL/kg/min) higher value associates with roughly 10–25% lower mortality (AHA, Circulation 2016). A higher or rising trend is a strong sign of cardiovascular health.
explainer.cardioRecoveryBody: Cardio recovery is how many beats per minute your heart rate falls in the first minute after you stop hard exercise. A bigger drop means your heart settles down faster, which is a sign of good fitness.
explainer.wristTemperatureBody: Wrist temperature is measured while you sleep and reported as the difference from your personal baseline, not an absolute value. A steady reading is normal, while a sustained rise can hint at illness, intense training, or a menstrual-cycle change.
explainer.fallsBody: This counts the hard falls your wearable detects. Zero is the goal; a rising count is a mobility-risk signal worth raising with a clinician, especially for older adults.
explainer.sixMinuteWalkBody: This is an estimate of how far you could walk in six minutes — a long-standing clinical test of mobility and cardiopulmonary endurance. The wearable figure is a proxy, not the supervised clinical test; a steady or rising trend is the useful read.
explainer.stairAscentSpeedBody: This is how fast you climb a flight of stairs. It is a sensitive marker of leg strength and mobility — a steady or rising pace is a good sign, and a falling one is worth watching.
explainer.stairDescentSpeedBody: This is how fast you go down a flight of stairs. Together with ascent speed it tracks leg control and balance — a steady pace is a good sign, and a falling one is worth watching.
explainer.breathingDisturbancesBody: A wearable can estimate how often your breathing was interrupted during sleep. A lower index is better; a level flagged as elevated, especially across many nights, is worth raising with a clinician. It is a screening proxy, not a sleep-apnea diagnosis.
explainer.stepsBody: Step count is the total number of steps you take each day. It is the simplest measure of everyday movement, and a steady habit matters more than any single big day. As general guidance, mortality risk falls steeply toward about 8,000 steps a day with continued benefit through roughly 12,000 (Saint-Maurice JAMA 2020); the optimal count is lower for older adults. The '10,000' figure is a marketing slogan, not research.
explainer.readinessBody: Readiness is a single daily read on how prepared your body is to take on the day, blending resting heart rate, heart-rate variability, sleep, and how steady your recent nights have been. It is a device proxy benchmarked against your own baseline, not a clinical measure, and scores are not comparable across devices. A higher score means recovered and resourced; a lower one is a gentle cue to ease off.
explainer.sleepScoreBody: Your sleep score distils a whole night into one number — duration, efficiency, regularity, and the balance of light, deep, and REM. Watch the pattern across weeks rather than chasing a perfect score on any single night.
explainer.recoveryBody: Recovery reflects how well your body has bounced back, drawing on overnight signals like heart-rate variability and resting heart rate. It is a device-derived proxy read against your own baseline, not a clinical measure, and is not comparable across devices. A strong score is a green light to push; a softer one suggests your system is still rebuilding.
explainer.strainBody: Strain captures how much cardiovascular and physical load you have put on your body, from workouts to an active day on your feet. It is a device-derived index judged against your own typical effort, not a clinical measure, and the headline number is not comparable across devices.
explainer.stressBody: Stress here is a device heuristic derived from heart-rate-variability patterns, not a cortisol measurement or how you consciously feel — and not a clinical stress score. Read it against your own baseline; it is not comparable across devices. A lower score points to a calm, well-regulated state; a higher one is an invitation to build in recovery.
explainer.recoveryPageBody: Recovery brings together the overnight signals your wearable records — recharge, strain, load and your day's heart-rate and energy totals — to show how well your body has bounced back. Read the pattern across days rather than any single number; a softer reading is an invitation to ease off, not an alarm.
explainer.pulsePressureBody: Pulse pressure is the gap between your systolic and diastolic readings (systolic minus diastolic). As general guidance, about 40 mmHg is typical at rest; a wide gap above roughly 60 mmHg in middle age can reflect stiffening arteries, while a narrow gap under about 25 mmHg can accompany low stroke volume (StatPearls/NIH 2023). It is a discussion prompt, not a diagnosis.
explainer.meanArterialPressureBody: Mean arterial pressure is the average pressure your organs see across a heartbeat, roughly the diastolic plus a third of the gap to systolic. As general guidance, a resting value sits around 70–100 mmHg (StatPearls/NIH 2023). The often-cited ~60 mmHg floor is an acute-care concept, not a consumer self-alarm.
explainer.cycleEducationBody: Your cycle runs through four phases driven by shifting hormones, and its length and regularity are an accessible window on overall health. As general guidance, adult cycles typically run 21–35 days (clinical references cite 24–38), with bleeding around five days (ACOG 2015). The luteal phase is the steady anchor, so ovulation is best estimated by counting back from your next expected period — never a fixed 'day 14'. A predicted fertile window is an estimate that needs several recorded cycles and stays approximate, and basal body temperature confirms ovulation after the fact rather than predicting it. This is not a contraceptive method.
explainer.sleepHygieneBody: A few evidence-based habits support sleep: keep consistent bed and wake times including weekends, get bright morning daylight and dim your evenings, keep the room cool, dark and quiet, and avoid afternoon caffeine, late alcohol and large late meals (CDC 2024). Regularity predicts health at least as strongly as duration, and a wearable's total sleep time is far more trustworthy than its deep, light and REM split.
explainer.referenceGuidance: General guidance, not medical advice. Reference ranges are population anchors that shift with your age, sex and health conditions — your own trend over time leads the read.

SLEEP_SCORE.title: Sleep score
SLEEP_SCORE.description: A transparent sleep-quality score with every sub-score shown.
SLEEP_SCORE.method: A 0–100 blend of sufficiency (asleep vs your age-based need), efficiency (asleep ÷ in-bed), consistency (how steady your sleep midpoint is), timing (vs your habitual midpoint) and composition (REM + deep share). Sub-scores reweight around any that your data cannot support; nothing is fabricated.

READINESS.title: Readiness
READINESS.description: A daily wellness proxy from how your signals sit against your own baseline.
READINESS.method: A 0–100 blend of resting-HR deviation, HRV (SDNN) deviation, sleep adequacy, respiratory-rate stability and mood stability — each measured against your personal baseline. Weights renormalise over the components your data supports. A wellness proxy, not a recovery or clinical score.
READINESS.caveat: A wellness proxy, not a recovery or clinical score.

VITALS_BASELINE.method: Your personal typical range is the median of your daily values over the window, ± a robust spread (median absolute deviation, scaled). It tracks YOUR readings, not a population norm — so a value inside the band is typical for you.

HRV_BALANCE.method: Your recent HRV (SDNN) average placed against your own rolling baseline (median ± a robust spread). Balanced means it sits inside your personal range; the band is built from your data, never a population cut-off.

BMI.method: Weight ÷ height², classified against the WHO adult categories (underweight / normal / overweight / obese). A screening ratio only — it does not distinguish muscle from fat.

FITNESS_AGE.method: Your VO₂max placed against age- and sex-referenced cardiorespiratory-fitness percentiles, expressed as a fitness-age delta. The VO₂max is your device's estimate — never recomputed here.

VASCULAR_AGE_DELTA.method: Your device's vascular-age / pulse-wave-velocity estimate compared with your chronological age, expressed as a delta in years. The estimate is the device's; HealthLog only re-frames it.
VASCULAR_AGE_DELTA.caveat: Your device's own estimate, re-framed — descriptive, not a clinical vascular assessment.
VASCULAR_AGE_DELTA.description: How your device's vascular-age estimate sits against your chronological age.

COINCIDENT_DEVIATION.method: A flag raised when two or more of your vitals sit outside their personal typical range on the same day. Each band is the robust median ± k·MAD of your own history, so it adapts to you rather than to a population cut-off.
COINCIDENT_DEVIATION.caveat: An awareness flag from your own baselines — descriptive, not a diagnosis or a clinical alert.

RECOVERY_SCORE.method: A nightly 0–100 recovery proxy blended from your HRV, resting heart rate and sleep against your own baseline. Higher means better-recovered. Computed once each night and read back here — never recomputed on the fly.
RECOVERY_SCORE.caveat: A descriptive wellness proxy, not a training-grade or clinical recovery measure.
RECOVERY_SCORE.description: A nightly recovery proxy from your HRV, resting heart rate and sleep.

STRESS_SCORE.method: A nightly 0–100 stress proxy from the shape of your intra-day HRV against your baseline. Higher means more physiological load.
STRESS_SCORE.caveat: An HRV-derived proxy, NOT an EDA / galvanic-skin stress measurement — your watch has no EDA sensor. Descriptive, not clinical.
STRESS_SCORE.description: A nightly stress proxy from the shape of your intra-day HRV.

STRAIN_SCORE.method: A nightly 0–100 strain proxy from your heart-rate-weighted activity load (a TRIMP-style training-impulse model). Higher means a harder day.
STRAIN_SCORE.anchorPersonal: Anchored to your own recent training-day load — scored relative to your typical effort, so a genuinely hard day for you reads high.
STRAIN_SCORE.anchorPopulation: Scored against a general reference while you build training history; once you have enough it switches to your own typical effort.
STRAIN_SCORE.caveat: A descriptive activity-load proxy, not a clinical or training-grade load measure.
STRAIN_SCORE.description: A nightly activity-load proxy from your heart-rate-weighted effort, relative to your typical effort.

WRIST_TEMPERATURE_BASELINE.method: Your typical overnight wrist temperature is the median of your nightly readings over the window, ± a robust spread (median absolute deviation, scaled). It tracks YOUR readings — last night is shown as a deviation from your own baseline, not a population norm.
WRIST_TEMPERATURE_BASELINE.caveat: A personal-deviation band only — descriptive, not an illness, fever or cycle-phase signal. Interpret it yourself.

STAIR_ASCENT_SPEED_BASELINE.method: Your typical stair-climbing pace is the median of your daily values over the window, ± a robust spread (median absolute deviation, scaled). The band is built from your data — there is no population stair-speed cut-off, because pace depends on stair geometry and leg length.
STAIR_ASCENT_SPEED_BASELINE.caveat: A personal mobility-trend band — descriptive, not a frailty or fall-risk assessment.

STAIR_DESCENT_SPEED_BASELINE.method: Your typical stair-descent pace is the median of your daily values over the window, ± a robust spread (median absolute deviation, scaled). The band is built from your data — there is no population stair-speed cut-off, because pace depends on stair geometry and leg length.
STAIR_DESCENT_SPEED_BASELINE.caveat: A personal mobility-trend band — descriptive, not a frailty or fall-risk assessment.

SIX_MINUTE_WALK_BAND.method: Your device's ESTIMATED six-minute-walk distance placed against the Enright & Sherrill reference for your age, height, weight and sex, as a percent of predicted. The distance is the device's estimate — never recomputed here. Without your age, height, weight and sex the percent is hidden and only the distance and trend are shown.
SIX_MINUTE_WALK_BAND.caveat: An estimate re-framed against a published reference — a functional-capacity awareness signal, not a clinical 6-minute-walk test or a diagnosis. The reference was established for ages 40–80; outside that range the predicted value is an extrapolation.

TRAJECTORY.method: A least-squares line fitted to your recent daily values and projected forward over a short horizon, with the textbook prediction interval that widens the further it reaches. Computed only when the recent trend is strong enough and your history is long and current enough; otherwise no line is drawn.
TRAJECTORY.caveat: A projection of your own recent trend — not a prediction, a target, or a clinical forecast. It holds only if the recent pattern continues; the shaded band shows how quickly that certainty fades.

gripStrength.title: Grip strength
gripStrength.description: Maximal handgrip force — a robust proxy for whole-body strength. Below the sex-specific cut-off (men 27 kg, women 16 kg) flags probable sarcopenia; your own trend leads the read.

pain.title: Pain (0–10)
pain.description: Patient-reported pain intensity on the 0–10 numeric rating scale, where 0 is no pain and 10 is the worst imaginable. A sustained 2-point change is the signal to watch.

emptyState.title: No pain scores yet
emptyState.description: Log how your pain feels right now on the 0–10 scale and the trend will appear here.

pain.coachPrefill: I haven't logged any pain scores yet — how should I track pain on the 0–10 scale, and what counts as a meaningful change?

waist.title: Waist circumference
waist.description: Waist girth in centimetres — a marker of central fat. The WHO increased-risk threshold is sex-specific (men 94 cm, women 80 cm); your own trend leads the read.

emptyState.title: No waist measurements yet
emptyState.description: Measure your waist at the midpoint between the lowest rib and the hip bone, at the end of a normal breath, and log it here.

waist.coachPrefill: I haven't logged any waist measurements yet — how do I measure my waist correctly, and what does the number mean for my health?

waistToHeight.title: Waist-to-height ratio
waistToHeight.description: Your waist divided by your height — the ancestry-robust single number. Keeping it under 0.5 (waist below half your height) is the simple target.

emptyState.title: No waist-to-height ratio yet
emptyState.description: Log a waist-to-height ratio and the trend will appear here. The rule of thumb: keep your waist under half your height.

waistToHeight.coachPrefill: I haven't logged a waist-to-height ratio yet — why is it a useful number, and how do I keep it under 0.5?

thresholds.overrideWarning: Overriding with values outside guideline ranges may produce unhealthy targets. Only do this on doctor's advice.

onboarding.errorGeneric: Something went wrong while finishing setup. Please try again.

dashboardOverview.title: Your dashboard
dashboardOverview.body: The dashboard pulls every module together at a glance. Long-press a tile to reorder or hide it (Settings → Dashboard layout). Each tile is only as telling as the data behind it — the modules ahead fill them.

quickAdd.title: Logged in seconds
quickAdd.body: Use “+ Add” to log any reading in seconds — weight, blood pressure, pulse, glucose. Hidden strength: backdate readings you forgot to enter. Everything you log here feeds trends, the health score, and the AI insights.

measurements.title: Readings & target ranges
measurements.body: Measurements shows the history and target range for each metric. Hidden gem: set a target range per value so deviations stand out in colour. These series are the basis for the Coach’s correlations and the doctor report.

medications.title: Medications & intakes
medications.body: Medications manages schedules, intakes, stock, and titration. Hidden: recurring plans (RRULE/rolling) and low-stock warnings. Your intake rate feeds the compliance pillar on the dashboard and the AI’s context.

labs.title: Lab values
labs.body: Labs captures biomarkers with reference ranges — blood count, cholesterol, HbA1c. Strength: import and FHIR export. Out-of-range lab values resurface as evidence in the insights and the doctor report.

illness.title: Illness journal
illness.body: The illness journal records acute episodes and chronic diagnoses with their flares. Hidden: correlate an episode with your readings, and Rest Mode that pauses reminders. That’s how the Coach understands why values drift for a while.

vorsorge.title: Checkups
vorsorge.body: Checkups remind you of screenings and vaccinations on their own interval. Tip: “Done” can create a reading or a lab result directly. Due items appear as reminders and in the doctor report.

cycle.title: Cycle
cycle.body: Cycle tracking logs phases and symptoms. Hidden: compare symptoms against mood and sleep. The cycle gives context for swings that would otherwise look unexplained.

mood.title: Mood
mood.body: Mood captures how you feel plus tags in two seconds. Strength: your own tags. Mood trends correlate with sleep, medications, and cycle — the Coach spots the patterns.

insights.title: Insights
insights.body: Insights is the heart of it: evidence-based, dynamic analysis instead of generic tips — every statement points at your real data. Hidden: the layout is customizable. Insights pulls together everything the modules above provide.

coach.title: Coach
coach.body: The Coach answers questions about your data in a dialogue, using your history, medications, and lab values as context. Pick your AI provider yourself (your own key or local). It recomputes nothing — it reads the same numbers as the dashboard.

integrations.title: Integrations
integrations.body: Integrations sync Withings, WHOOP, Polar, Oura, and Apple Health automatically. Weight, sleep, and recovery fill in without manual typing — and feed the insights and Coach in turn.

export.title: Doctor report
export.body: The doctor report bundles readings, medications, lab values, and checkups as PDF or FHIR for the appointment. This is where every module converges — a complete report only comes from well-kept data.

achievements.title: Achievements
achievements.body: Achievements reward consistency — streaks and milestones. A small motivation loop that makes sticking with it easier.

wrapUp.title: That’s the tour
wrapUp.body: You can revisit any module on its own at any time (“Show this tour” on every page) or restart the whole tour under Settings → Advanced.

goals.title: What would you like to track?
goals.body: Pick the areas you care about — we'll surface them first on your dashboard, and they help you decide what to set up next.
goals.helpHint: One to three priorities tend to work best — you can always add more later.

source.title: Connect a source
source.body: Bring in measurements automatically, or stay on manual entry. Nothing here is configured for you — each card just shows where to connect it.

manual.title: Manual entry
manual.body: Log readings yourself, whenever it fits. Works on any device, no third-party account required.

withings.title: Withings
withings.body: Scales, blood-pressure cuffs and trackers from Withings sync automatically.

more.title: More sources
more.body: Connect any of these in Settings whenever you're ready.

baseline.title: A bit about you
baseline.body: Four optional fields sharpen target ranges, BMI and reminders. You can adjust any of them later in Settings.
baseline.displayNameHint: Shown on the dashboard greeting and in reports.

done.title: You're all set
done.body: Welcome aboard. Your dashboard is ready.
done.learning: Some insights — glucose, sleep rhythm, blood-pressure trends, cycle — sharpen over the first week or two as your data builds up. Empty tiles now just mean we're still learning your baseline.
done.aiBody: Coach, the daily briefing and assessments turn your readings into plain language. For that you connect your own AI key (OpenAI, Anthropic) or a local model — your data stays with you.

welcomeBack.title: Welcome back
welcomeBack.body: You've already finished onboarding. To adjust anything, head to Settings — everything you set up is editable from there.

disclaimer.acknowledge: I understand: HealthLog is a private tracking tool, not a clinical assessment or diagnosis. If I'm worried I'll talk to my doctor, not this app.

slide1.title: Track every signal
slide1.body: Weight, blood pressure, glucose, sleep, mood — keep your full picture in one log you own.

slide2.title: Connect your sources
slide2.body: Sync with Apple Health and Withings, or add readings by hand. Your data stays on your server.

slide3.title: See the pattern
slide3.body: Daily summaries, trend charts, and a coach that surfaces what changed — all on your own device.

anamnesis.privacyNote: Stored encrypted and used only to give you better context. We don't ask for your medications here — add those anytime from the Medications page.

home.title: Home location
home.description: A coarse, city-level location — never a precise track. Weather and daylight are fetched for this place on days no location period applies, from the date you set it onward.
home.since: Weather tracked from {date} onward. Earlier days stay empty until you add a location period.

travel.title: Location periods
travel.description: Add a place and a date range — a trip, or a stretch of earlier history. The period overrides your home for the days it covers, so weather matches where you actually were. This is how you correct the past.

backfill.title: Backfill past days
backfill.description: Fetch and store weather for a past date range so older days join the correlations. The start defaults to your home's effective date; to reach further back, add a location period for that range first.

coachMemory.title: What the Coach remembers
coachMemory.description: Durable facts you have told the Coach. It uses these to keep its replies relevant. Remove anything you would rather it forgot.

intro.title: Your data stays yours
intro.body: HealthLog is self-hosted. This page brings together how your data is protected, how long it is kept, how to get it out, and how to delete it.

encryption.description: Sensitive fields are encrypted in the database.

delete.title: Delete or reset
delete.description: Remove your account entirely, or wipe your health records while keeping the account.
delete.backupLag: Deletion removes your data immediately. Encrypted off-host backups still contain it until they roll off the operator's backup retention window.

posture.title: Privacy posture
posture.description: Control how much your data is shared with AI features and opt-in research.

mcp.enableDescription: Let an MCP-capable assistant (Claude.ai, ChatGPT) read your health data over a scoped token. Off by default.
mcp.connectDescription: Paste this URL into your assistant's custom-connector setup, then authorize with the token below or sign in when prompted.
mcp.tokensDescription: Mint a read-only token to connect an assistant. The token is shown once.
mcp.scopeNote: Tokens are scoped to health:read — read-only access to your own records. They can never reach admin functions.
mcp.writeScopeNote: Adds the health:write scope so the assistant can log new measurements and mood entries to your account. Every write is confirmed first. The token still can't delete or edit existing entries, change medications, or reach REST or admin functions. Leave off for read-only access.
mcp.connectionsDescription: Assistants you authorized through the sign-in flow. Revoking a connection ends its access immediately, including its ability to refresh.
mcp.connectionRevokeDescription: This ends the assistant's access immediately and stops it from renewing. It will need to be re-authorized to reconnect.

phaseEducation.title: What's happening now

whatsHappening.MENSTRUAL: Your period is the start of a new cycle, as the uterine lining sheds. Energy and mood often feel lower in these first days.
whatsHappening.FOLLICULAR: After your period, oestrogen gradually rises as the body prepares an egg. Many people notice steadier energy through this stretch.
whatsHappening.OVULATORY: Around mid-cycle an egg is released and fertility peaks for a short window. This phase is the briefest of the four.
whatsHappening.LUTEAL: After ovulation, progesterone rises and the body settles in. In the days before your next period some people notice premenstrual changes.

context.MENSTRUAL: Hormone levels are at their lowest, which is why these days can feel like a quiet reset. Some people notice cramps, lighter sleep or a wish to slow down; others sail through with little change. All of that sits within the normal range, and how it feels can shift from one cycle to the next.
context.FOLLICULAR: With oestrogen on the rise, many people describe these days as bright and open — sleep often comes more easily and the mind feels clearer. Basal body temperature usually stays in its lower band until just before ovulation. The length of this phase varies most from person to person, and from cycle to cycle.
context.OVULATORY: Oestrogen peaks just before the egg is released, often alongside a brief lift in mood and a small rise in basal body temperature in the day or two that follow. Cervical fluid tends to become clearer and more stretchy around this window. A short twinge on one side is something some people notice; many feel nothing at all, and both are perfectly normal.
context.LUTEAL: Progesterone leads the second half and basal body temperature usually settles a little higher than earlier in the cycle. Some people notice shifts in sleep, appetite, mood or energy as the days go on, while others feel steady throughout. The pattern is personal, and it often looks different from one month to the next.

desc.total-cholesterol: Total cholesterol is the sum of the cholesterol carried across all lipoprotein particles in the blood, including the LDL, HDL, and remnant fractions. Cholesterol itself is an essential building block for cell membranes, hormones, and bile acids. As a single number it is a coarse summary, because both a healthy HDL share and a high LDL share lift the total — so it is usually read alongside the individual fractions. It is tracked mainly as part of long-term cardiovascular risk assessment.
desc.ldl: LDL cholesterol is the cholesterol carried by low-density lipoprotein particles, the fraction that delivers cholesterol to tissues but can also deposit it in artery walls. Over years this deposition contributes to atherosclerotic plaque, which is why LDL is one of the most closely watched lipid markers. Lower long-term exposure generally tracks with lower cardiovascular risk. It is interpreted by risk category rather than against one fixed normal value.
desc.hdl: HDL cholesterol is the cholesterol carried by high-density lipoprotein particles, which collect surplus cholesterol from tissues and return it to the liver for recycling or removal. Because of this transport role it is often described as the protective fraction. Very low levels tend to accompany higher cardiovascular risk, though raising it artificially has not reliably reduced risk, so it is read as a marker rather than a target. It is usually interpreted together with LDL and triglycerides.
desc.triglycerides: Triglycerides are the main form of fat circulating in the blood, supplied both by the diet and produced by the liver, and they serve as a key energy store. Levels rise after meals, so the sample is usually taken fasting for comparability. Persistently high triglycerides are associated with metabolic and cardiovascular risk and often travel with insulin resistance and low HDL. They are read as one component of the overall lipid picture.
desc.fasting-glucose: Fasting glucose is the concentration of sugar in the blood measured after an overnight fast, reflecting how the body holds its baseline blood-sugar level without recent food. Glucose is the body's primary immediate fuel, and insulin normally keeps it within a narrow range. A fasting measurement removes the effect of recent meals, making it a standard screen for how glucose regulation is functioning. It is one of the markers used to assess metabolic health over time.
desc.hba1c: HbA1c is the share of haemoglobin in red blood cells that has become bound to glucose, which accumulates in proportion to average blood-sugar exposure. Because red cells live for about three months, it reflects average glucose over the preceding two to three months rather than a single moment. This makes it a stable, meal-independent view of glucose control. Its accuracy can be affected by conditions that change red-cell lifespan.
desc.tsh: TSH (thyroid-stimulating hormone) is released by the pituitary gland to signal the thyroid to produce its hormones, and it adjusts up or down to keep them in balance. Because of this feedback loop, TSH is the most sensitive first-line screen of thyroid function: it tends to move before the thyroid hormones themselves drift. A raised or lowered TSH points toward an under- or over-active thyroid and usually prompts measurement of the thyroid hormones directly. Pregnancy and age shift the expected range.
desc.ft3: Free T3 (triiodothyronine) is the unbound, biologically active form of the more potent thyroid hormone, the fraction available to act on tissues. Most circulating T3 is bound to carrier proteins and inactive, so the free portion is what reflects true hormone availability. It is measured to characterise thyroid function, especially alongside TSH and free T4 when results are unclear. Levels are strongly assay-dependent and read in clinical context.
desc.ft4: Free T4 (thyroxine) is the unbound form of the main hormone the thyroid releases into the blood, serving as a reservoir that tissues convert to the more active T3 as needed. The free fraction reflects what is actually available to the body, independent of carrier-protein levels. It is usually measured together with TSH to separate thyroid-gland problems from pituitary signalling. Pregnancy and certain medications can shift it.
desc.ferritin: Ferritin is the body's main iron-storage protein, and its blood level broadly mirrors total iron reserves. A low value points toward depleted stores, while a high value can reflect iron overload — or simply inflammation, because ferritin also rises as an acute-phase reactant. For that reason it is often interpreted alongside other iron markers and signs of inflammation. It is one of the core measures used to assess iron status.
desc.transferrin-saturation: Transferrin saturation is the percentage of transferrin — the protein that ferries iron through the blood — that is currently loaded with iron. It links the amount of iron in transit to the body's iron-carrying capacity, giving a snapshot of iron availability that complements ferritin's view of storage. A low saturation suggests iron is in short supply, while a high value can indicate overload. It is read together with ferritin and the full iron panel.
desc.vitamin-d: Vitamin D is measured as 25-hydroxyvitamin D, the circulating storage form and the standard marker of overall vitamin D status. The body makes it in skin exposed to sunlight and absorbs some from food, and it supports calcium balance, bone health, and immune function. Levels vary with season, latitude, skin tone, and supplementation. It is tracked to gauge whether stores are adequate over time.
desc.vitamin-b12: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is an essential vitamin obtained mainly from animal-source foods and stored largely in the liver. It is required for red-blood-cell formation, DNA synthesis, and the health of the nervous system. Prolonged shortfall — from low intake or impaired absorption — can lead to anaemia and nerve-related symptoms. It is measured to assess nutritional status, particularly where intake or absorption may be limited.
desc.folate: Folate (vitamin B9) is a B-vitamin found in leafy greens, legumes, and fortified foods, central to red-blood-cell formation and DNA synthesis. It works closely with vitamin B12, and a shortfall in either can produce a similar type of anaemia. Adequate folate is especially important around early pregnancy for healthy fetal development. It is measured to assess nutritional status and the cause of certain anaemias.
desc.hs-crp: High-sensitivity CRP measures C-reactive protein, a marker the liver releases in response to inflammation, using an assay refined to detect the low concentrations relevant to cardiovascular risk. Unlike standard CRP used for acute infection, the high-sensitivity version captures the subtle, chronic inflammation linked to vascular disease. Because any recent infection or injury can raise it, it is interpreted away from acute illness and often confirmed with a repeat sample. It is read as one contributor to overall cardiovascular risk.
desc.creatinine: Creatinine is a waste product of normal muscle metabolism that the kidneys filter out of the blood at a fairly steady rate. Because production is relatively constant, its blood level serves as a practical gauge of how well the kidneys are clearing waste. Values are also influenced by muscle mass, hydration, and diet, so it is usually interpreted alongside the estimated filtration rate. It is a core marker of kidney function.
desc.egfr: eGFR (estimated glomerular filtration rate) estimates how much blood the kidneys filter each minute, calculated from creatinine together with age and sex. It translates a raw creatinine value into a more directly interpretable measure of kidney function. A declining eGFR over time is the main way chronic kidney changes are staged and tracked. As an estimate it is read as a trend rather than a single exact figure.
desc.alt: ALT (alanine aminotransferase) is an enzyme concentrated in liver cells, released into the blood when those cells are stressed or damaged. Because it is relatively liver-specific, it is one of the primary markers used to detect liver injury or inflammation. Levels can rise with fatty liver, viral hepatitis, alcohol, or certain medications. It is usually read together with AST and the other liver-panel markers.
desc.ast: AST (aspartate aminotransferase) is an enzyme found in the liver but also in muscle, heart, and red blood cells, released when any of those tissues is injured. Because it is less liver-specific than ALT, the two are interpreted together — their ratio can hint at the source and nature of the problem. Vigorous exercise or muscle injury can raise it independently of the liver. It is one of the standard liver-panel enzymes.
desc.ggt: GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase) is an enzyme found in the liver and the bile ducts that rises particularly when bile flow is obstructed or with alcohol exposure. It is often used to clarify why another liver marker is elevated — for example, to distinguish a liver or bile-duct source. It is sensitive but not very specific, so it is read in context with the rest of the liver panel. It is one of several markers used to assess liver and biliary health.
desc.sodium: Sodium is the principal electrolyte in the fluid outside the body's cells and the main driver of fluid balance and blood volume. The body regulates it tightly through thirst, hormones, and the kidneys, so its blood level reflects the balance between water and salt more than dietary intake alone. Abnormal values usually signal a disturbance of water balance rather than of sodium itself. It is a core part of the basic metabolic and electrolyte panel.
desc.potassium: Potassium is the main electrolyte inside the body's cells and is essential for nerve signalling and the steady rhythm of the heart muscle. Its blood level is held within a narrow range by the kidneys and hormonal signals, because both high and low values can disturb the heart's electrical activity. Levels can be affected by kidney function, hydration, and several common medications. It is a core measure in the electrolyte panel.
desc.hemoglobin: Haemoglobin is the iron-containing protein in red blood cells that binds oxygen in the lungs and carries it to the tissues. Its concentration is the main measure of the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity and the central marker for detecting anaemia. Low values can reflect blood loss, iron or vitamin shortfalls, or reduced production, while high values can follow dehydration or low-oxygen conditions. It is a cornerstone of the complete blood count.
desc.hematocrit: Haematocrit is the proportion of blood volume made up of red blood cells, expressed as a percentage. It moves closely with haemoglobin and gives a complementary view of red-cell mass and the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity. Hydration affects it, since plasma-volume changes shift the proportion even when red-cell numbers are stable. It is read alongside haemoglobin and the rest of the blood count.
desc.wbc: The white blood cell count is the total number of immune cells circulating in the blood, the body's mobile defence against infection. It rises in response to infection, inflammation, or stress and can fall when the bone marrow is suppressed or in some immune conditions. Because it sums several distinct cell types, a notable change is often followed up with a differential that breaks it down. It is a core component of the complete blood count.
desc.platelets: Platelets are small cell fragments in the blood that clump together and form the initial plug that stops bleeding from a damaged vessel. Their count reflects the balance between production in the bone marrow and consumption or breakdown. A low count can raise bleeding tendency, while a high count can follow inflammation or other stimuli. It is part of the complete blood count and a basic gauge of clotting capacity.
desc.apob: Apolipoprotein B is the structural protein carried by each of the atherogenic lipoprotein particles, so measuring it counts those particles directly rather than the cholesterol they carry. Because one ApoB molecule sits on each LDL, VLDL, and related particle, it can capture cardiovascular risk that an LDL-cholesterol value misses — particularly when particle number and cholesterol content diverge. For this reason it is increasingly used to refine lipid-based risk assessment. It is interpreted alongside the standard lipid panel.
desc.lp-a: Lipoprotein(a) is an LDL-like particle with an extra attached protein, and its level is largely set by genetics and stays fairly stable through life. Elevated Lp(a) is an independent, inherited risk factor for cardiovascular disease and aortic-valve narrowing. Because it changes little, it is usually measured once to establish a person's lifelong risk rather than tracked repeatedly. It is reported in molar units and read as part of overall cardiovascular risk.
desc.fasting-insulin: Fasting insulin is the level of insulin — the hormone that moves glucose from the blood into cells — measured after an overnight fast. Paired with fasting glucose, it helps estimate insulin resistance, the state in which the body needs more insulin to keep glucose in range. A high fasting insulin alongside normal glucose can be an early sign that this compensation is under way. It is read as part of a metabolic-health picture rather than in isolation.
desc.omega-3-index: The omega-3 index is the share of EPA and DHA — the two long-chain marine omega-3 fatty acids — among the fatty acids in red-blood-cell membranes. Because red cells turn over slowly, it reflects long-term omega-3 intake rather than a recent meal. A higher index has been associated in observational studies with lower cardiovascular risk. It is tracked as a stable marker of omega-3 status that responds to diet over months.

selfDescription.intendedPurpose: HealthLog is a personal health record-keeping and wellness tool. It helps you log, visualise, and export your own health data. It does not diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any disease, and it is not a medical device.
selfDescription.coach: The Coach works from your own logged data. It isn't a doctor and doesn't give medical advice — for diagnosis or treatment, talk to a healthcare professional.
selfDescription.insights: These are descriptive patterns computed from your own logged data, not a clinical assessment or diagnosis.
selfDescription.labs: These are your own records, not a medical diagnosis. Reference ranges vary by lab and person — only your clinician can interpret them for you.
selfDescription.medications: This tracks the medications you log and your own adherence. It doesn't assess dosing or interactions and isn't medical advice — confirm those with a pharmacist or doctor.
selfDescription.emergency: If this might be an emergency, contact your local emergency number or a clinician now. HealthLog can't help in an emergency.
selfDescription.dataPosture: HealthLog is self-hosted: your health data stays on your own instance, encrypted at rest. AI features use a provider key you bring or a local model, so nothing leaves your instance beyond the provider you configure, and there is no third-party analytics on your health data.
